payload.title: World Cup Winner
payload.description:
This market will resolve according to the national team that wins the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

If at any point it becomes impossible for this team to win the FIFA World Cup based on the rules of FIFA (e.g., they are eliminated in the knockout stage), this market will resolve immediately to “No”.

If the 2026 FIFA World Cup is permanently canceled or has not been completed by October 13, 2026, 11:59 PM this market will resolve to “Other”.

The primary resolution source will be official information from FIFA, however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Spain, France, and England sit atop the World Cup winner market as the expanded 48-team tournament opens in days across North America, their narrow leads reflecting deep squads, strong recent form, and consistent tournament pedigree. Spain enters as European champions with standout young talent driving offensive depth, while France leverages its world ranking, 2018 title experience, and broad roster options under long-time leadership. England builds on back-to-back European final appearances and flawless qualifying results. The close clustering of probabilities underscores how evenly matched these sides appear on paper, with South American contenders like Argentina and Brazil facing steeper paths despite their own histories, and no decisive late injuries or form shifts separating the frontrunners.

payload.title: Will any World Cup game scheduled in the U.S. be relocated abroad?
payload.description:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is scheduled to begin on Thursday, June 11, and conclude on Sunday, July 19. More information about the match schedule can be found here (https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/match-schedule-fixtures-results-teams-stadiums). According to ESPN, there have been complaints about the summertime heat across America, causing FIFA to consider relocating the 2026 World Cup out of the United States.

This market will resolve to “Yes” if it is announced that any game originally scheduled to be held in the U.S. to be relocated to a location outside of the U.S. by June 10, 2026, 11:59 ET. Otherwise, it will resolve to “No”.

A qualifying announcement will immediately resolve the market to "Yes" regardless of whether the relocation is later revoked. 

The resolution source will be official information from FIFA; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: FIFA has repeatedly affirmed that all 78 matches scheduled across the 11 U.S. host cities will proceed as planned, most recently rejecting formal requests—including from Iran—to shift games to Mexico or elsewhere. Organizers have completed extensive infrastructure upgrades, security deployments, and venue preparations in cities such as Los Angeles, New York/New Jersey, and Dallas, with the tournament opener set for June 11 in Mexico City and U.S. fixtures following immediately. Despite earlier political commentary and isolated calls for adjustments over immigration or heat concerns, senior FIFA and local officials have dismissed relocation scenarios as unrealistic given the fixed co-hosting agreement with Canada and Mexico. This sustained official stance underpins the overwhelming trader consensus reflected in the 97.5% implied probability for no relocation. Extreme unforeseen developments, such as a sudden widespread security crisis or natural disaster, remain theoretically possible but have shown no supporting indicators this close to kickoff.

eventMetadata.context_description: Spain, France, and England sit atop the World Cup winner market as the expanded 48-team tournament opens in days across North America, their narrow leads reflecting deep squads, strong recent form, and consistent tournament pedigree. Spain enters as European champions with standout young talent driving offensive depth, while France leverages its world ranking, 2018 title experience, and broad roster options under long-time leadership. England builds on back-to-back European final appearances and flawless qualifying results. The close clustering of probabilities underscores how evenly matched these sides appear on paper, with South American contenders like Argentina and Brazil facing steeper paths despite their own histories, and no decisive late injuries or form shifts separating the frontrunners.

body.title: Will any World Cup game scheduled in the U.S. be relocated abroad?
body.description:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is scheduled to begin on Thursday, June 11, and conclude on Sunday, July 19. More information about the match schedule can be found here (https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/match-schedule-fixtures-results-teams-stadiums). According to ESPN, there have been complaints about the summertime heat across America, causing FIFA to consider relocating the 2026 World Cup out of the United States.

This market will resolve to “Yes” if it is announced that any game originally scheduled to be held in the U.S. to be relocated to a location outside of the U.S. by June 10, 2026, 11:59 ET. Otherwise, it will resolve to “No”.

A qualifying announcement will immediately resolve the market to "Yes" regardless of whether the relocation is later revoked. 

The resolution source will be official information from FIFA; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: FIFA has repeatedly affirmed that all 78 matches scheduled across the 11 U.S. host cities will proceed as planned, most recently rejecting formal requests—including from Iran—to shift games to Mexico or elsewhere. Organizers have completed extensive infrastructure upgrades, security deployments, and venue preparations in cities such as Los Angeles, New York/New Jersey, and Dallas, with the tournament opener set for June 11 in Mexico City and U.S. fixtures following immediately. Despite earlier political commentary and isolated calls for adjustments over immigration or heat concerns, senior FIFA and local officials have dismissed relocation scenarios as unrealistic given the fixed co-hosting agreement with Canada and Mexico. This sustained official stance underpins the overwhelming trader consensus reflected in the 97.5% implied probability for no relocation. Extreme unforeseen developments, such as a sudden widespread security crisis or natural disaster, remain theoretically possible but have shown no supporting indicators this close to kickoff.
